steps.title: Keep a record of every item

narration.default: The shopping bills you've built so far throw away each item right after adding it to the total — once the loop is over, only the final number is left. Today you'll keep a record of every item the kid added, by growing a list as the loop runs. By the end, your program will print the total AND show every name in the basket.

Type the opening:

print("Store Your Items")

n = int(input("How many items? "))

Familiar territory — title and count. The new variable comes next.

Type:

items = []
total = 0

The square brackets [] make an empty list — like a row of empty boxes, ready to be filled. items is a list, total is a single number. Both start empty/zero. As the loop runs, items will grow and total will climb.

steps.title: Three questions per item, then store the name

narration.default:
Type:

for i in range(n):
    name = input("Enter item name: ")
    price = int(input("Enter price: "))
    qty = int(input("Enter quantity: "))

    items.append(name)

    total = total + (price * qty)

The loop body asks three questions: name (text), price (number), quantity (number). items.append(name) adds the name to the END of the list — append is a list method, standard Python. The list grows by one each loop.

Lists are different from variables. A variable holds ONE value at a time — assigning a new value forgets the old. A list holds MANY values in order, and append adds a new one without forgetting the rest. After three appends with apple, banana, mango, items will look like ['apple', 'banana', 'mango'].

steps.title: Print the basket and the bill

narration.default:
After the loop ends — back to no indent — type:

print("Items bought:", items)
print("Total Bill:", total)

The first line prints the whole list at once — Python knows how to display a list neatly with the names in square brackets. The second line prints the total. Two snapshots of what just happened: the contents and the cost.

Click Run. Type 3 items. Add apple at 10 each, qty 2. Add bread at 30, qty 1. Add milk at 50, qty 1. The terminal prints Items bought: ['apple', 'bread', 'milk'] and Total Bill: 100. The list remembered every name in the order you typed them.

Lists are one of Python's most useful tools. You can store quiz questions, costume names for a sprite, levels in a game, students in a class, even other lists. Python has more list tricks built in — counting how many things are in a list, finding the biggest, sorting them — each in one short word.
